slides.title: Passing Data In 📥
slides.content:
# Parameters Make Functions Flexible

```python
def greet(name):  # name is a parameter
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")

greet("Alice")    # "Alice" is an argument
```

* **Parameter** — Variable in definition
* **Argument** — Value when calling

slides.title: Parameters in Action 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
def greet(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")

def add(a, b):
    print(f"{a} + {b} = {a + b}")

greet("Python")
greet("World")
add(5, 3)
add(10, 20)

quizData.question: In def greet(name), what is 'name'?
quizData.explanation: In the definition, 'name' is a parameter — a variable that will receive a value.

slides.title: Multiple Parameters 📋
slides.content:
# Any Number of Parameters

```python
def introduce(name, age, city):
    print(f"I'm {name}, {age}, from {city}")

introduce("Alice", 25, "Paris")
```

Separate parameters with commas!

slides.title: Default Values 🎁
slides.content:
# Parameters Can Have Defaults

```python
def greet(name, greeting="Hello"):
    print(f"{greeting}, {name}!")

greet("Alice")           # Hello, Alice!
greet("Bob", "Hi")       # Hi, Bob!
```

Default parameters are optional!

slides.title: Default Values 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
def power(base, exponent=2):
    result = base ** exponent
    print(f"{base}^{exponent} = {result}")

power(5)        # Uses default: 5^2
power(2, 10)    # Override: 2^10
power(3, 3)     # 3^3

quizData.question: In def f(x, y=10), what's y if I call f(5)?
quizData.explanation: y has a default of 10, so if not provided, y = 10!

slides.title: Keyword Arguments 🔑
slides.content:
# Name Your Arguments

```python
def profile(name, age, city):
    print(f"{name}, {age}, {city}")

# Keyword arguments (any order!)
profile(city="Paris", name="Alice", age=25)
```

Keyword arguments make code clearer!

slides.title: Keyword Args 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
def send_email(to, subject, body, urgent=False):
    status = "🔴 URGENT" if urgent else "📧"
    print(f"{status} To: {to}")
    print(f"Subject: {subject}")
    print(f"Body: {body[:20]}...")

# Positional
send_email("bob@email.com", "Hi", "Hello there!")
print()
# Keyword (clearer!)
send_email(
    to="alice@email.com",
    subject="Meeting",
    body="Let's meet tomorrow",
    urgent=True
)

quizData.question: What's the benefit of keyword arguments?
quizData.explanation: Keyword arguments make code clearer and let you pass them in any order!

slides.title: *args: Variable Arguments 📦
slides.content:
# Accept Any Number of Arguments

```python
def add_all(*numbers):
    return sum(numbers)

add_all(1, 2)          # 3
add_all(1, 2, 3, 4, 5) # 15
```

`*args` collects all positional arguments into a tuple!

slides.title: *args Example 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
def print_all(*items):
    print(f"Received {len(items)} items:")
    for item in items:
        print(f"  - {item}")

print_all("apple")
print()
print_all("apple", "banana", "cherry", "date")

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## **kwargs for Keyword Arguments

```python
def config(**settings):
    for key, value in settings.items():
        print(f"{key} = {value}")

config(theme="dark", lang="en")
```

`**kwargs` collects keyword args into a dictionary!

quizData.question: What does *args create?
quizData.explanation: *args collects all positional arguments into a tuple!
